content:
# Leviticus 15 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 15 (33 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Total disagreements:** 38

---

## Summary

Leviticus 15 is a technically dense chapter with four parallel legislative units. The three agents agree broadly on structure and major vocabulary but diverge significantly on: (1) handling of the technical terms zav/zavah/niddah/shkhvat-zera (whether to transliterate, gloss, or render by function); (2) several interpretively significant cruxes, especially v. 3 (rur/hechtim — "flows/retains" or "flows/ceases") and v. 19 (opening clause structure, LXX's insertion of "blood," and the niddah gloss); (3) register differences throughout — PNEUMA consistently shifts to casual or medical register ("healed," "monthly period," "leather," "smashed") while EMET tends to hyper-literal constructions; (4) the v. 31 purpose-statement rendering; and (5) the closing summary verses (vv. 32–33). The most consequential disagreements are on v. 3 (hechtim), v. 13 (tahar as "cleansed" vs. "healed"), v. 19 (niddah gloss and PNEUMA's added glosses), and the name/term conventions throughout.

---

## Disagreement 1 — V. 1: Name forms — "Moshe and Aharon" vs. "Moses and Aaron"

**Verse:** 1
**Hebrew:** אֶל-מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל-אַהֲרֹן (el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon)
**EMET:** "to Moshe and to Aharon"
**LOGOS:** "to Moses and to Aaron"
**PNEUMA:** "to Moses and Aaron"
**Ruling:** "to Moses and to Aaron"
**Type:** Name convention
**Notes:** EMET uses Hebrew transliterations throughout as part of its literal register. LOGOS and PNEUMA use the established English forms. Project convention (established from Genesis onward): English name forms in all consensus text. PNEUMA drops the second "to" (ve-el); the MT has the repeated preposition el before each name, which LOGOS correctly renders. Consensus: "to Moses and to Aaron" follows LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 2 — V. 1: "saying" — with or without

**Verse:** 1
**Hebrew:** לֵאמֹר (lemor)
**EMET:** includes "saying" at end of v. 1
**LOGOS:** includes "saying"
**PNEUMA:** omits "saying" — runs directly into v. 2 as a single speech
**Ruling:** Include "saying"
**Type:** Structural / formula
**Notes:** The lemor formula ("saying") is the standard speech-introduction in Levitical legislation and marks the boundary between the divine-to-Moses/Aaron level and the Moses/Aaron-to-Israel level. PNEUMA's omission collapses this structure. EMET and LOGOS correctly retain it. The dual address (to Moses and Aaron) is the same as Lev 13:1; the lemor is present there also and should be consistent.

---

## Disagreement 3 — V. 2: zav — transliterate or render functionally

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** זָב (zav)
**EMET:** "a discharge [zav]" — preserves the term with extensive in-text notes
**LOGOS:** "the one who has the discharge" — functional rendering, no transliteration in text body
**PNEUMA:** "the man with the discharge" — no transliteration
**Ruling:** "discharge [zav — זָב, one with an abnormal genital discharge]" at first occurrence; "the one with the discharge" thereafter
**Type:** Technical term / transliteration policy
**Notes:** EMET's full preservation of zav is appropriate for its register but the in-text LXX notes are not carried into the consensus. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render the term functionally. The project convention for technical terms is a bracketed gloss at first occurrence (cf. niddah, ketonet passim, hesed); the zav is a category-term used throughout chs. 15, 22 and is worth flagging once. Thereafter "the one with the discharge" is the clearest formal equivalent.

---

## Disagreement 4 — V. 2: mibsaro — "from his body" vs. "from his flesh"

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** מִבְּשָׂרוֹ (mibsaro — "from his flesh / from his body")
**EMET:** "from his flesh"
**LOGOS:** "from his body"
**PNEUMA:** "from his body"
**Ruling:** "from his body"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Basar means "flesh, body." EMET's "flesh" is the more literal rendering of basar. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "body," which is the more idiomatic English for the priestly euphemism (the discharge comes from the genital region, referred to euphemistically as besaro). The LXX uses σώματος (sōmatos, "body"). "Body" is the established rendering in this project for basar in the context of priestly euphemism; ruling follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## Disagreement 5 — V. 3: hechtim — "retained/sealed" vs. "stopped" vs. "stops up"

**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** הֶחְתִּים בְּשָׂרוֹ (hechtim besaro — "his body has retained / sealed")
**EMET:** "or his body has retained his discharge"
**LOGOS:** "or his body is stopped from his discharge"
**PNEUMA:** "or his body stops up the discharge"
**LXX (Vaticanus):** εἴτε ἐπιλέλοιπεν (eite epileloipen — "or has ceased")
**Ruling:** "or his body retains his discharge"
**Type:** Interpretive crux / word choice
**Notes:** This is the most significant exegetical disagreement in the chapter's opening section. The Hebrew hechtim is the hiphil of chatam ("to seal, to stop up, to block"). EMET correctly identifies the root: chatam = "to seal," so hechtim = "has caused to be sealed / has retained." The picture is of the discharge being sealed/retained inside: both the active flow and the retained, intermittent condition render the man unclean. LOGOS's "stopped" and PNEUMA's "stops up" both follow the LXX (ἐπιλέλοιπεν, "has ceased/left off") — reading hechtim as the cessation of the flow. The MT's hechtim (root chatam) and the LXX's ἐπιλείπω (to cease, to fail) represent genuinely different understandings. The consensus follows the MT's hechtim: "retains" — the condition of the zav encompasses both active discharge and the retention/blocking of the discharge. The contrast is not between "flowing now" and "flowing later stopped" but between two persistent states of the condition.

---

## Disagreement 6 — V. 4: ha-keli / "implement" vs. "everything"

**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** כָּל-הַכְּלִי (kol-ha-keli — "every implement / every vessel")
**EMET:** "every implement on which he sits"
**LOGOS:** "everything on which he sits"
**PNEUMA:** "everything he sits on"
**Ruling:** "every implement on which he sits"
**Type:** Word choice / vocabulary precision
**Notes:** The Hebrew keli is a technical term throughout Leviticus meaning "vessel, implement, object" — more specific than "everything." The chapter carefully distinguishes mishkav (lying-surface), keli yoshev (sitting-implement), and merkav (riding-implement). Using "implement" preserves this technical vocabulary. LOGOS and PNEUMA's "everything" is broader and less precise. The LXX uses σκεῦος (skeuos, "vessel/implement"), supporting "implement." Ruling: "every implement."

---

## Disagreement 7 — V. 5: formula rendering — "shall" vs. "must" / "will"

**Verses:** 5–12 (formula repetitions)
**Hebrew:** וְטָמֵא עַד-הָעֶרֶב (ve-tame ad ha-erev — "and shall be unclean until the evening")
**EMET:** "shall wash... shall bathe... shall be unclean"
**LOGOS:** "shall wash... shall bathe... shall be unclean"
**PNEUMA:** "must wash... bathe in water... and will be unclean"
**Ruling:** "shall wash... and bathe in water... and shall be unclean until the evening"
**Type:** Modal / register
**Notes:** PNEUMA consistently renders the imperfect verbs of the laundering-bathing-until-evening formula as "must... will" — a present-tense imperative register. EMET and LOGOS both use "shall," the standard modal for legal prescription in English translations of Levitical law. "Shall" better captures the jussive/imperfect legal-directive sense of the Hebrew. PNEUMA's shift to "must/will" is appropriate for dynamic translation but out of place in consensus legal prose. The formula is repeated across vv. 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 21, 22, 27; the ruling applies uniformly.

---

## Disagreement 8 — V. 9: merkav — "saddle" vs. "riding-implement"

**Verse:** 9
**Hebrew:** מֶרְכָּב (merkav)
**EMET:** "riding-implement"
**LOGOS:** "saddle"
**PNEUMA:** "saddle"
**Ruling:** "saddle"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Merkav (from rakhav, "to ride") denotes a riding-implement broadly — a saddle or pack-saddle. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "saddle," which is the most accessible English equivalent. EMET's "riding-implement" is accurate but less natural. The LXX ἐπίσαγμα (episagma, "pack-saddle") is slightly different from LOGOS's "saddle" but close. Ruling: "saddle" for clarity.

---

## Disagreement 9 — V. 11: "without having rinsed" — perfect vs. simple present

**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** וְיָדָיו לֹא-שָׁטַף בַּמָּיִם (ve-yadav lo shataph ba-mayim — "and his hands he has not rinsed in water")
**EMET:** "without rinsing his hands in water"
**LOGOS:** "without having rinsed his hands in water"
**PNEUMA:** "without having rinsed his hands in water"
**Ruling:** "without having rinsed his hands in water"
**Type:** Verbal aspect / grammar
**Notes:** The Hebrew shataph is a perfect form indicating a prior action (or lack thereof): the zav has not rinsed his hands before touching. The perfect precondition is better captured by "without having rinsed" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) than by "without rinsing" (EMET, which implies contemporaneous action). LOGOS and PNEUMA agree. Ruling: "without having rinsed."

---

## Disagreement 10 — V. 12: yishaver — "broken" vs. "smashed"

**Verse:** 12
**Hebrew:** יִשָּׁבֵר (yishaver — niphal of shavar, "shall be broken")
**EMET:** "shall be broken"
**LOGOS:** "shall be broken"
**PNEUMA:** "must be smashed"
**Ruling:** "shall be broken"
**Type:** Word choice / register
**Notes:** The niphal of shavar = passive "shall be broken." EMET and LOGOS agree. PNEUMA's "must be smashed" (a) shifts to "must" (see Disagreement 7) and (b) uses "smashed," which is more emphatic and colloquial than the Hebrew warrants. "Broken" is the standard equivalent for shavar in priestly law.

---

## Disagreement 11 — V. 13: yihar — "cleansed" vs. "healed"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** כִּי-יִטְהַר הַזָּב מִזּוֹבוֹ (ki-yihar ha-zav mi-zo'vo — "when the zav is cleansed from his discharge")
**EMET:** "when the zav is cleansed from his discharge"
**LOGOS:** "when the one who has the discharge is cleansed of his discharge"
**PNEUMA:** "when the man with the discharge is healed of his discharge"
**Ruling:** "when the one with the discharge is cleansed of his discharge"
**Type:** Word choice / register — significant
**Notes:** The Hebrew yihar is from tahar ("to be/become clean, to be ritually cleansed") — a purity-status term, not a healing term. PNEUMA's "healed" imports a medical register not present in the Hebrew. The verse describes a change in ritual status (from tameh to tahor), not necessarily the medical cure of a physical condition (though the medical and ritual may coincide). The legislating priests of Leviticus are careful to distinguish between healing and cleansing; tahar here is ritual cleansing. EMET and LOGOS both correctly use "cleansed." Ruling: "cleansed" — LOGOS's full formulation is most natural.

---

## Disagreement 12 — V. 13: mayim chayyim — "fresh water" vs. "living water"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** מַיִם חַיִּים (mayim chayyim — "living water / fresh/running water")
**EMET:** "living water"
**LOGOS:** "fresh water"
**PNEUMA:** "fresh water"
**Ruling:** "fresh water"
**Type:** Technical term
**Notes:** The Hebrew mayim chayyim literally = "living water." EMET preserves the literal image. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "fresh water," rendering the functional meaning (running or spring water, as opposed to stored cistern water). The project established "fresh water" as the ruling for this term in LEV_014 (and the same term appears in the tzara'at cleansing there). Consistency with that ruling confirmed here. The LXX uses ὕδωρ ζῶν (hydōr zōn, "living water") — preserving the literal; the consensus nevertheless follows "fresh water" for functional clarity.

---

## Disagreement 13 — V. 14: "two young pigeons" — LOGOS drops "young"

**Verse:** 14
**Hebrew:** שְׁנֵי בְנֵי-יוֹנָה (shnei benei yonah — "two sons of the dove / two young pigeons")
**EMET:** "two young doves"
**LOGOS:** "two pigeons"
**PNEUMA:** "two young pigeons"
**Ruling:** "two young pigeons"
**Type:** Word choice / completeness
**Notes:** Benei yonah = "sons of the dove" = young pigeons (the same term as bney yonah in LEV_012, v. 8, where the ruling was "young pigeons"). LOGOS drops "young," losing the ben ("son/young of") element. EMET says "doves" for yonah, which is not standard — yonah in this context typically renders as "pigeon" (in contrast to tor, "turtledove"). PNEUMA correctly renders "young pigeons." Ruling: "two young pigeons" — consistent with LEV_012, v. 8.

---

## Disagreement 14 — V. 19: Opening clause structure

**Verse:** 19
**Hebrew:** וְאִשָּׁה כִּי-תִהְיֶה זָבָה דָּם יִהְיֶה זֹבָהּ בִּבְשָׂרָהּ (ve-ishah ki-tihyeh zo'vah, dam yihyeh zo'vah bi-vesarah — "and a woman, when she is discharging, her discharge in her body is blood")
**EMET:** "And a woman — when her discharge is blood in her flesh — she shall be in her niddah"
**LOGOS:** "And if a woman has a discharge, and her discharge in her body is blood, she shall be in her menstrual impurity"
**PNEUMA:** "When a woman has her regular discharge — her monthly period — the uncleanness of her flow will last seven days"
**Ruling:** "And when a woman has a discharge, and her discharge in her body is blood, she shall be in her menstrual impurity [niddah — נִדָּה] for seven days"
**Type:** Structural / interpretive — significant
**Notes:** PNEUMA makes two unwarranted additions: (1) "regular discharge" — the MT does not say "regular"; the distinction between regular (niddah) and irregular (zavah) is the point of this section, but the MT simply says "a discharge ... is blood"; (2) "her monthly period" — a gloss not in the MT, substituting a contemporary clinical term for the technical Hebrew niddah. Both additions are interpretive smoothings that remove the reader from the text's own legal framing. LOGOS and EMET both follow the MT's two-clause structure. Consensus follows LOGOS's structure.

---

## Disagreement 15 — V. 19: niddah gloss — transliterate vs. render functionally vs. omit

**Verse:** 19
**Hebrew:** בְּנִדָּתָהּ (be-niddat'ah — "in her niddah")
**EMET:** "in her niddah [נִדָּה]" with extensive inline notes
**LOGOS:** "in her menstrual impurity [niddah]"
**PNEUMA:** no transliteration — "the uncleanness of her flow"
**Ruling:** "in her menstrual impurity [niddah — נִדָּה]"
**Type:** Technical term / transliteration policy
**Notes:** PNEUMA omits the niddah gloss entirely and does not transliterate the term at any point in the chapter. This is a significant loss: niddah is the technical legal term that controls chs. 15, 18, 20 and is a key term of the entire holiness legislation. LOGOS correctly supplies the gloss at first occurrence. EMET's in-text notes are not reproduced in the consensus body. Ruling: "menstrual impurity [niddah — נִדָּה]" with the Hebrew character preserved, following project convention for technical terms.

---

## Disagreement 16 — V. 19: "everyone who touches her" — gender-neutral vs. masculine pronoun

**Verse:** 19
**Hebrew:** וְכָל-הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּהּ (ve-khol-ha-nogea' bah — "and everyone who touches her")
**EMET:** "and everyone who touches her"
**LOGOS:** "And whoever touches her shall be unclean until the evening"
**PNEUMA:** "Anyone who touches her will be unclean until evening"
**Ruling:** "And everyone who touches her shall be unclean until the evening"
**Type:** Minor / pronoun
**Notes:** The Hebrew ha-nogea' is a masculine participle used generically. EMET uses "everyone" (inclusive), LOGOS uses "whoever" (inclusive), PNEUMA uses "anyone" (inclusive). All are acceptable; "everyone" (EMET) is most literal (kol = "all, everyone"). LOGOS's "whoever" is the most legal-English in register. Ruling: "everyone who touches her" — EMET's "everyone" most closely mirrors kol.

---

## Disagreement 17 — V. 20: "during her menstrual impurity" — PNEUMA omits qualifier

**Verse:** 20
**Hebrew:** בְּנִדָּתָהּ (be-niddat'ah — "in/during her niddah")
**EMET:** "during her niddah"
**LOGOS:** "during her menstrual impurity"
**PNEUMA:** "during her period of uncleanness"
**Ruling:** "during her menstrual impurity"
**Type:** Technical term consistency
**Notes:** PNEUMA drops niddah entirely and substitutes "period of uncleanness" — a general term that does not preserve the legal category. LOGOS and EMET preserve the niddah term (rendered in English). The ruling on niddah as "menstrual impurity" (with transliteration at first occurrence only) applies throughout. Subsequent occurrences in the chapter use "menstrual impurity" without the bracketed term.

---

## Disagreement 18 — V. 23: Verse structure — PNEUMA runs v. 23 into v. 22

**Verse:** 23
**Hebrew:** וְאִם עַל-הַמִּשְׁכָּב הוּא אוֹ עַל-הַכְּלִי אֲשֶׁר-הִיא יֹשֶׁבֶת עָלָיו... (ve-im al-ha-mishkav hu' o al-ha-keli asher-hi yoshevet alav...)
**EMET:** separate verse: "And if it is on the bed ... or upon the implement upon which she sits — when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening"
**LOGOS:** separate verse: "Whether it is the bed or anything on which she sits, when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening"
**PNEUMA:** runs v. 23 into the v. 22 paragraph: "Whether it is something on the bed or something she sat on — when anyone touches it, they will be unclean until evening"
**Ruling:** Separate verse, following LOGOS
**Type:** Structural
**Notes:** PNEUMA merges vv. 22–23 into a single paragraph. EMET and LOGOS maintain the MT's verse boundary. Consensus retains verse boundaries throughout. LOGOS's rendering of v. 23 is the clearest: "Whether it is the bed or anything on which she sits, when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening."

---

## Disagreement 19 — V. 24: shakhov yishkav — emphatic infinitive absolute

**Verse:** 24
**Hebrew:** וְאִם שָׁכֹב יִשְׁכַּב אִישׁ אֹתָהּ (ve-im shakhov yishkav ish ottah — "and if, lying, a man lies with her")
**EMET:** "and if a man does indeed lie with her" — captures the doubled infinitive emphasis
**LOGOS:** "And if any man lies with her" — drops the emphasis
**PNEUMA:** "If a man actually lies with her" — captures the emphasis informally
**Ruling:** "And if a man does indeed lie with her"
**Type:** Grammatical form / emphasis
**Notes:** The MT uses the cognate infinitive absolute (shakhov yishkav) for emphasis — a common Hebrew device signaling deliberateness or moral weight. EMET and PNEUMA both attempt to render this emphasis; LOGOS drops it. "Does indeed lie" (EMET) is appropriate in a legal context. PNEUMA's "actually" is too colloquial. Ruling: "does indeed lie with her."

---

## Disagreement 20 — V. 24: niddat'ah alav — "menstrual impurity comes upon him" vs. "niddah is upon him" vs. "menstrual flow touches him"

**Verse:** 24
**Hebrew:** וּתְהִי נִדָּתָהּ עָלָיו (u-tehi niddat'ah alav — "and her niddah shall come to be upon him")
**EMET:** "and her niddah is upon him"
**LOGOS:** "and her menstrual impurity comes upon him"
**PNEUMA:** "and her menstrual flow touches him"
**LXX (Vaticanus):** καὶ γένηται ἡ ἀκαθαρσία αὐτῆς ἐπ' αὐτόν (kai genētai hē akatharsia autēs ep' auton — "and her uncleanness comes upon him")
**Ruling:** "and her menstrual impurity comes upon him"
**Type:** Word choice / idiom rendering
**Notes:** The Hebrew tehi (from hayah, "to be/become") + niddat'ah alav = "her niddah comes to be upon him." This is an idiomatic expression for the transmission of niddah-impurity through intercourse. LOGOS captures the dynamic correctly: "comes upon him." EMET's "is upon him" is static. PNEUMA's "touches him" softens the idiom significantly (there is no touching here — the transmission is by the discharge contacting the man during intercourse). The LXX uses "her uncleanness comes upon him" (γένηται, "comes to be"). Ruling: "comes upon him" (LOGOS).

---

## Disagreement 21 — V. 25: Structure of the two-condition clause

**Verse:** 25
**Hebrew:** בְּלֹא עֶת-נִדָּתָהּ אוֹ כִי-תָזוּב עַל נִדָּתָהּ (be-lo' et niddat'ah o ki-tazov al niddat'ah — "not at the time of her niddah, or if she discharges beyond her niddah")
**EMET:** "not at the time of her niddah, or when she has a discharge beyond her niddah"
**LOGOS:** "not at the time of her menstrual impurity, or if she has a discharge beyond the time of her menstrual impurity"
**PNEUMA:** "outside the time of her monthly period — or when her discharge continues beyond her period"
**Ruling:** "not at the time of her menstrual impurity, or if she has a discharge beyond her menstrual impurity"
**Type:** Structure / technical term
**Notes:** PNEUMA substitutes "monthly period" for niddah twice in this verse and also drops the precise Hebrew prepositions (be-lo' et, "not at the time of" and al, "beyond/above"). LOGOS accurately follows the two-condition structure of the MT. "Beyond her menstrual impurity" (not "beyond the time of her menstrual impurity") is chosen for concision; "the time of" is implied by the preposition context and is not present in the Hebrew al niddat'ah. EMET preserves niddah; consensus uses "menstrual impurity."

---

## Disagreement 22 — V. 25: zo'vah tum'at'ah — "unclean discharge"

**Verse:** 25
**Hebrew:** זוֹבָהּ טֻמְאָתָהּ (zo'vah tum'at'ah — "her discharge of her impurity")
**EMET:** "her discharge of her impurity"
**LOGOS:** "the discharge of her uncleanness"
**PNEUMA:** "her impure discharge"
**Ruling:** "her unclean discharge"
**Type:** Word order / phrase structure
**Notes:** The Hebrew has zo'vah ("her discharge") + tum'at'ah ("her impurity") in apposition — a genitive construction. LOGOS's "discharge of her uncleanness" and PNEUMA's "her impure discharge" are both defensible. EMET's "her discharge of her impurity" is more literal. Consensus: "her unclean discharge" — the most natural English, capturing zo'vah (discharge) and tum'at'ah (impurity / unclean) without redundancy. The same construction in v. 30 (me-zo'vat tum'at'ah) is rendered "her unclean discharge" consistently.

---

## Disagreement 23 — V. 26: ke-mishkav niddat'ah — "as the bed of her menstrual impurity" vs. "period bed"

**Verse:** 26
**Hebrew:** כְּמִשְׁכַּב נִדָּתָהּ (ke-mishkav niddat'ah — "as the bed of her niddah")
**EMET:** "as the bed of her niddah"
**LOGOS:** "as the bed of her menstrual impurity"
**PNEUMA:** "as if it were her period bed"
**Ruling:** "as the bed of her menstrual impurity"
**Type:** Register / technical term
**Notes:** PNEUMA's "period bed" is colloquial. The legal register of the chapter requires "as the bed of her menstrual impurity" (LOGOS). EMET's use of niddah without gloss is appropriate in its register; the consensus uses the English equivalent at this point (niddah was glossed at v. 19).

---

## Disagreement 24 — V. 28: ve-achar tit'har / "afterward she shall be clean"

**Verse:** 28
**Hebrew:** וְאַחַר תִּטְהָר (ve-achar tit'har — "and afterward she shall be clean")
**EMET:** "and afterward she shall be clean"
**LOGOS:** "and after that she shall be clean"
**PNEUMA:** "after that she will be clean"
**Ruling:** "and afterward she shall be clean"
**Type:** Minor / adverb rendering
**Notes:** Ve-achar = "and afterward." EMET's "afterward" is the most direct equivalent. LOGOS's "after that" is slightly wordy. PNEUMA's "will be clean" shifts from the jussive/imperfect "shall" to the future "will" — inconsistent with the legal register. Ruling: "afterward she shall be clean" (EMET).

---

## Disagreement 25 — V. 29: "bring them to the priest" — word order

**Verse:** 29
**Hebrew:** וְהֵבִיאָה אוֹתָם אֶל-הַכֹּהֵן אֶל-פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ve-hevi'ah otam el-ha-kohen el-petach ohel mo'ed)
**EMET:** "she shall bring them to the priest to the entrance of the tent of meeting"
**LOGOS:** "she shall take for herself two turtledoves or two young pigeons and bring them to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting"
**PNEUMA:** "she shall take two turtledoves or two young pigeons and bring them to the priest at the entrance to the tent of meeting"
**Ruling:** "and bring them to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting"
**Type:** Minor / preposition
**Notes:** The three agents agree substantially; the minor difference is "to the entrance" vs. "at the entrance." The Hebrew el-petach = "to the entrance of." Both are idiomatic; the project has consistently used "at the entrance of the tent of meeting" in Leviticus (cf. LEV_012, v. 6). Ruling: "at the entrance of the tent of meeting."

---

## Disagreement 26 — V. 30: alav / "for him" vs. "for her" — gender of atonement pronoun

**Verse:** 30
**Hebrew:** וְכִפֶּר עָלֶיהָ הַכֹּהֵן (MT reads aleha — "for her" in most MSS; EMET notes some MSS read alav — "for him")
**EMET:** notes the MT has the masculine alav in some MSS but the feminine aleha in most; translates "and the priest shall make atonement over/for her" — follows the feminine
**LOGOS:** "And the priest shall make atonement for her before the LORD" (feminine, follows standard MT)
**PNEUMA:** "So the priest shall make atonement before the LORD for her" (feminine)
**LXX (Vaticanus):** περὶ αὐτῆς (peri autēs — feminine: "for her")
**Ruling:** "for her" (feminine)
**Type:** Textual / gender
**Notes:** EMET correctly notes a textual variant but all agents translate with the feminine pronoun, which is the correct reading in the MT (aleha) and the LXX (αὐτῆς). The masculine alav in some MSS is a scribal assimilation to the masculine default of the formula in v. 15. The Leningrad Codex reads aleha (feminine) at v. 30. All three agents agree on the feminine; the ruling confirms "for her."

---

## Disagreement 27 — V. 31: ve-hizartem — "you shall separate" vs. "keep separate" vs. "keep... separate"

**Verse:** 31
**Hebrew:** וְהִזַּרְתֶּם (ve-hizartem — hiphil of nazar, "cause to be separated / keep separate")
**EMET:** "and you shall separate"
**LOGOS:** "Thus you shall separate"
**PNEUMA:** "You shall thus keep the people of Israel separate from their impurities"
**Ruling:** "Thus you shall separate"
**Type:** Word choice / verb rendering
**Notes:** The hiphil of nazar = "to cause to be separated, to keep at a distance, to keep separate." All three agents capture this. LOGOS's "Thus you shall separate" is the most fluent opening for the purpose-statement verse. PNEUMA substitutes "people of Israel" for bney Yisra'el (see Disagreement 28). EMET's "and you shall separate" loses the summarizing "Thus."

---

## Disagreement 28 — V. 31: bney Yisra'el — "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel"

**Verse:** 31
**Hebrew:** אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (et-bney Yisra'el)
**EMET:** "the sons of Yisra'el"
**LOGOS:** "the sons of Israel"
**PNEUMA:** "the people of Israel"
**Ruling:** "the sons of Israel"
**Type:** Address formula — standard project ruling
**Notes:** PNEUMA's "people of Israel" for bney Yisra'el is the same dynamic equivalence choice ruled against in LEV_012 (Disagreement 2) and consistently throughout this series. The project ruling is "sons of Israel" throughout. LOGOS and EMET agree; ruling confirmed.

---

## Disagreement 29 — V. 31: mishkani — "my tabernacle" vs. "my dwelling place"

**Verse:** 31
**Hebrew:** אֶת-מִשְׁכָּנִי (et-mishkani — "my tabernacle / my dwelling-place")
**EMET:** "my Tabernacle"
**LOGOS:** "my tabernacle"
**PNEUMA:** "my dwelling place"
**Ruling:** "my tabernacle"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Mishkan = "dwelling-place, tabernacle" (from shakhan, "to dwell"). All three agents render the term; the difference is "tabernacle" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "dwelling place" (PNEUMA). The project has established "tabernacle" as the standard rendering for mishkan throughout Leviticus (cf. LEV_008–LEV_014 series). PNEUMA's "dwelling place" is accurate but loses the established term. Ruling: "tabernacle" — LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 30 — V. 32: ha-torah — "instruction" vs. "law" vs. "Torah"

**Verse:** 32
**Hebrew:** זֹאת תּוֹרַת (zot torat — "this is the instruction / law of")
**EMET:** "the Torah of the one who has a discharge"
**LOGOS:** "the law for the one who has a discharge"
**PNEUMA:** "the instruction for the man with a discharge"
**Ruling:** "the instruction for the one with a discharge"
**Type:** Key term / established project ruling
**Notes:** The project ruling for torah in summary-formula contexts is "instruction" (established in LEV_012, v. 7 and confirmed at Lev 11:46, 13:59, 14:2, 32, 54, 57). EMET preserves the Hebrew term; LOGOS uses "law" (standard formal equivalence, not the project ruling); PNEUMA correctly uses "instruction." Ruling: "instruction" — PNEUMA. The summary formula zot torat ha-X appears at vv. 32–33 as the colophon for the entire chapter; "instruction" is the ruling term.

---

## Disagreement 31 — V. 32: la-zav — "one with a discharge" vs. "man with a discharge"

**Verse:** 32
**Hebrew:** לַזָּב (la-zav — "of/for the zav")
**EMET:** "of the one who has a discharge"
**LOGOS:** "the one who has a discharge"
**PNEUMA:** "the man with a discharge"
**Ruling:** "the one with a discharge"
**Type:** Word choice / gender specificity
**Notes:** The zav is a male category, so "man" (PNEUMA) is not inaccurate, but the legal summary (vv. 32–33) lists all four categories together and then provides the closing gender-inclusive formula "male or female" (v. 33). Using "the one" (EMET, LOGOS) is more consistent with the legal summary's structure, which relies on the category terms, not gender specifications within each item (gender is addressed in v. 33's closing formula).

---

## Disagreement 32 — V. 32: le-tame'ah-bo — "becoming unclean by it" vs. "to become unclean"

**Verse:** 32
**Hebrew:** לְטָמְאָה-בוֹ (le-tame'ah-bo — "to become unclean by it")
**EMET:** "to become unclean by it"
**LOGOS:** "becoming unclean by it"
**PNEUMA:** "becomes unclean from it"
**Ruling:** "becoming unclean by it"
**Type:** Grammatical form
**Notes:** LOGOS's participial "becoming unclean by it" flows naturally in the summary sentence structure. EMET's infinitive "to become" is grammatically precise. PNEUMA's "becomes" (present tense) is weakest in the legal register. The consensus sentence reads: "for the one from whom there goes out an emission of seed, becoming unclean by it" — LOGOS's participial is most natural here.

---

## Disagreement 33 — V. 33: ha-davah be-niddat'ah — "sick in her menstrual impurity" vs. "in the uncleanness of her monthly period"

**Verse:** 33
**Hebrew:** הַדָּוָה בְּנִדָּתָהּ (ha-davah be-niddat'ah — "the one ailing / sick one in her niddah")
**EMET:** "the one ailing in her niddah"
**LOGOS:** "her who is sick in her menstrual impurity"
**PNEUMA:** "the woman in the uncleanness of her monthly period"
**LXX (Vaticanus):** τῆς ἀδιαθέτου ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς ("of the indisposed/unwell one in her separation")
**Ruling:** "the one who is sick in her menstrual impurity [niddah]"
**Type:** Word choice / technical term — significant
**Notes:** Ha-davah (from davah, "to be sick, faint, weak") is not simply a synonym for niddah; it is a separate term emphasizing the physical dimension of the menstruating woman's condition — she is ailing, weakened. The LXX's ἀδιάθετος ("indisposed, unwell") confirms this nuance. PNEUMA drops ha-davah entirely, using only "the woman in the uncleanness of her monthly period" — losing both the davah element and again substituting "monthly period" for niddah. LOGOS's "sick in her menstrual impurity" captures both elements. EMET is precise but uses niddah without gloss. Consensus: "the one who is sick in her menstrual impurity [niddah]" — supplies the bracketed term because this is the final occurrence of niddah in the chapter's summary verse, and the term is being used in the legal colophon.

---

## Disagreement 34 — V. 33: ve-la-zav — second zav in summary

**Verse:** 33
**Hebrew:** וְלַזָּב (ve-la-zav — "and for the one with a discharge")
**EMET:** "and of the one having a discharge"
**LOGOS:** "and for anyone, male or female, who has a discharge"
**PNEUMA:** "and for any man or woman with a discharge"
**Ruling:** "and for anyone — male or female — who has a discharge"
**Type:** Structure — significant
**Notes:** LOGOS and PNEUMA both expand the ve-la-zav in v. 33 to include the "male or female" phrase from la-zakhar ve-la-neqevah (which follows immediately in the MT). EMET keeps them as separate clauses. The MT structure of v. 33 is a list: (1) the davah in her niddah, (2) the one having a discharge, (3) the male, (4) the female, (5) the man who lies with an unclean woman. The Hebrew la-zakhar ve-la-neqevah ("for the male and for the female") is a qualifying phrase that attaches to the zav — i.e., the discharge can be male or female. LOGOS correctly reads the structure by combining ve-la-zav with la-zakhar ve-la-neqevah. This reading produces: "for anyone — male or female — who has a discharge," which is the proper interpretation of the verse's structure and follows the reading of LOGOS and PNEUMA. EMET's reading separates them, suggesting the male and female are independent list items (which gives v. 33 five items rather than four). Ruling: LOGOS's combined reading.

---

## Disagreement 35 — V. 33: la-zakhar ve-la-neqevah — "male or female" vs. "man or woman"

**Verse:** 33
**Hebrew:** לַזָּכָר וְלַנְּקֵבָה (la-zakhar ve-la-neqevah — "for the male and for the female")
**EMET:** "of the male and of the female"
**LOGOS:** "male or female"
**PNEUMA:** "man or woman"
**Ruling:** "male or female"
**Type:** Word choice / legal vocabulary
**Notes:** Zakhar/neqevah are the sex-category terms (as established throughout ch. 12 and confirmed in the LEV_012 rulings on vv. 2, 5, 7). "Male or female" preserves the legal register of the sex-terms. PNEUMA's "man or woman" is the social register (social persons rather than sex-category). LOGOS: "male or female." Ruling: "male or female" — consistent with LEV_012.

---

## Disagreement 36 — V. 33: im-temei'ah — "a woman who is unclean" vs. "an unclean woman"

**Verse:** 33
**Hebrew:** עִם-טְמֵאָה (im-temei'ah — "with the unclean one / with an unclean woman")
**EMET:** "with the unclean one"
**LOGOS:** "with a woman who is unclean"
**PNEUMA:** "with an unclean woman"
**Ruling:** "with a woman who is unclean"
**Type:** Word choice / minor
**Notes:** The Hebrew temei'ah is the feminine form of tameh ("unclean"), functioning here as a noun: "the unclean woman." EMET's "the unclean one" is precise (feminine participle used as noun) but slightly ambiguous in English. LOGOS's "a woman who is unclean" is clear and maintains the predicative structure. PNEUMA's "an unclean woman" uses the adjective attributively, which is natural but slightly different from the Hebrew's noun. Ruling: "a woman who is unclean" (LOGOS) — the predicative construction preserves the Hebrew's nominal form more accurately.

---

## Disagreement 37 — Structural: PNEUMA merges several verse blocks

**Verses:** 1–4, 5–8, 19–23
**Type:** Structural / format
**Notes:** PNEUMA merges multiple verse groups into flowing paragraphs without hard verse breaks within those paragraphs: vv. 1–4 run as a single block; vv. 5–8 run as a single block; vv. 19–23 run as a single block. EMET and LOGOS maintain verse-by-verse formatting throughout. The consensus maintains separate numbered verses for all 33 verses. PNEUMA's paragraph-flow structure, while appropriate for dynamic translation, prevents verse-level reference and loses the formulaic parallel structure that is one of the chapter's most important literary features. The repetition of the laundering-bathing-until-evening formula in each successive verse is not accidental; it is a deliberate legal architecture, and running the verses together obscures this.

---

## Disagreement 38 — V. 3 / V. 19: LXX addition of "blood" in v. 19

**Verse:** 19
**Hebrew:** וְאִשָּׁה כִּי-תִהְיֶה זָבָה (ve-ishah ki-tihyeh zo'vah — "and a woman, when she is discharging") — the substance is named in the following clause (dam = blood)
**LXX (Vaticanus):** γυνὴ ἥτις ἐὰν ῥέῃ αἷμα (gynē hētis ean rhēē haima — "a woman who flows blood") — LXX inserts αἷμα ("blood") into the opening clause
**EMET:** follows MT sequence (zo'vah without "blood" in first clause)
**LOGOS:** follows MT sequence
**PNEUMA:** not directly relevant (PNEUMA adds "regular discharge" and "monthly period" instead — a different set of glosses)
**Ruling:** Follow MT sequence; do not bring "blood" into the first clause
**Type:** LXX variant / MT vs. LXX
**Notes:** The LXX Vaticanus anticipates the second clause by inserting αἷμα ("blood") into the first clause, producing "a woman who flows blood." The MT places the identification of the substance (dam, "blood") in the second clause: "her discharge in her body is blood." The MT's two-clause structure is more precise, distinguishing the discharge-condition (zo'vah) from the identification of the substance (dam). The consensus follows the MT's sequence. All three agents follow the MT on this point (though PNEUMA replaces the clause structure with other glosses).

---

## MT vs. LXX Divergence Summary

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Impact on Consensus |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 3 | hechtim ("retained/sealed") | ἐπιλέλοιπεν ("ceased") | Significant: MT has internal retention; LXX has cessation of flow. Consensus follows MT. |
| 9 | merkav ("saddle/riding-implement") | ἐπίσαγμα ("pack-saddle") | Minor: both rendered as "saddle." |
| 13 | mayim chayyim ("living water") | ὕδωρ ζῶν ("living water") | LXX agrees with MT; consensus uses "fresh water" per project ruling. |
| 19 | zo'vah (she flows — substance not yet named) | ῥέῃ αἷμα ("flows blood" — LXX adds "blood" to first clause) | LXX anticipates the second clause. Consensus follows MT sequence. |
| 24 | niddat'ah alav ("her niddah upon him") | ἡ ἀκαθαρσία αὐτῆς ἐπ' αὐτόν ("her uncleanness upon him") | LXX uses "uncleanness" rather than "niddah." MT's niddah is a more specific legal term. Consensus: "menstrual impurity." |
| 30 | alav (masc., some MSS) / aleha (fem., majority MSS) | αὐτῆς (fem.) | LXX confirms the feminine reading. Consensus: "for her." |
| 31 | ve-hizartem (hiphil of nazar, "cause to separate") | καὶ ἐκτενεῖτε ("you shall keep away") / some MSS: ἀφοριεῖτε ("shall set apart") | LXX varies; both renderings convey the separation idea. Consensus: "you shall separate" following MT root. |
| 33 | ha-davah be-niddat'ah ("the ailing one in her niddah") | τῆς ἀδιαθέτου ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς ("the indisposed one in her separation") | LXX confirms the davah ("ailing, indisposed") element. Consensus preserves "sick in her menstrual impurity." |
